threats.title: setLockDuration Allows Premature Unlock via Flawed Validation Check
threats.hypothesis_description: setLockDuration validates the new duration using `block.timestamp + _duration >= unlockTime` but computes the actual new unlockTime as `lastLockTime + _duration`. When significant time has elapsed since locking, a user can pass the validation with a small duration that sets unlockTime to a past timestamp, enabling immediate withdrawal of all locked tokens while retaining lockdrop NFT rewards.
threats.investigation_rationale: The validation check and the state assignment use fundamentally different base timestamps (block.timestamp vs lastLockTime), creating an exploitable gap that grows with elapsed time. After waiting >= 50% of the original lock duration, any user can bypass remaining lock time entirely. This directly violates the project's stated invariant that users must not reduce lockup times.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Validation: `uint32(block.timestamp) + uint32(_duration) < unlockTime` but assignment: `unlockTime = lastLockTime + uint32(_duration)`. Different base timestamps.

preconditions: User has locked tokens with quantity > 0
Sufficient time has elapsed since lastLockTime (>= D_old/2 for immediate unlock)
Contract is not paused

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Check uses block.timestamp as base but assignment uses lastLockTime — divergence grows linearly with time
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None — any user with locked tokens can exploit
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Available after elapsed_time >= original_duration - MaxLockDuration, practically after ~50% of lock period

operational_questions: What is the minimum wait time for exploitation given MaxLockDuration config?
Can this be chained with lockdrop NFT rewards to extract value?
Does forceHarvest before unlock capture undeserved schnibbles?
validation_requirements: Deploy contract, lock tokens for 365 days, advance time 183 days, call setLockDuration(183 days), verify unlockTime is in the past
Confirm immediate unlock succeeds after setLockDuration exploit
Verify NFT rewards are retained after early unlock

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Lock tokens during lockdrop for maximum duration to maximize NFT rewards
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Call setLockDuration(unlockTime - block.timestamp) — passes check since block.timestamp + duration >= unlockTime
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: New unlockTime = lastLockTime + (unlockTime - block.timestamp) << block.timestamp. Call unlock() immediately.
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None — single user, single transaction sequence

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Lockdrop integrity destroyed — users can commit to long locks, receive rewards, then exit early
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All lock/unlock operations, lockdrop reward fairness, TVL metrics

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Basic — requires understanding of the math discrepancy

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: setLockDuration calls followed by immediate unlock
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Users unlocking before their original unlockTime
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Lock during lockdrop, reduce duration mid-period, unlock early

threats.title: lockOnBehalf Enables Permanent Fund Freezing via Zero-Cost Unlock Time Reset
threats.hypothesis_description: lockOnBehalf allows any caller to lock 0 tokens (ETH with msg.value=0, or ERC20 with _quantity=0) on behalf of any registered user. The _lock function unconditionally overwrites unlockTime = block.timestamp + lockDuration for the recipient, resetting their countdown without adding any value. An attacker can repeatedly call this to permanently prevent a victim from ever unlocking their tokens.
threats.investigation_rationale: _lock sets unlockTime without checking whether the recipient's existing unlockTime is being extended. With 0 quantity, the attacker pays only gas cost. There is no minimum quantity check, no recipient consent mechanism, and no check that new unlockTime >= old unlockTime. This directly violates the project's stated concern: 'Funds must not get locked forever.'

proof_of_signal.pattern: Unconditional overwrite: `lockedToken.unlockTime = uint32(block.timestamp) + uint32(_lockDuration)` with no check against existing unlockTime and no minimum quantity requirement

preconditions: Victim has locked tokens with quantity > 0
Victim's token contract is active and configured
Victim is a registered main account
Contract is not paused

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: lockOnBehalf accepts 0 quantity, still overwrites unlockTime
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None — any address can call lockOnBehalf
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent — attacker can repeat indefinitely at gas-only cost

operational_questions: Does transferFrom(attacker, contract, 0) succeed for all configured tokens?
Can forceHarvest revert and block the attack for some recipients?
Is there any off-chain monitoring for lockOnBehalf calls?
validation_requirements: Call lockOnBehalf(tokenContract, 0, victim) and verify victim's unlockTime is reset
Confirm victim cannot unlock despite original unlockTime having passed
Test with both ETH (address(0)) and ERC20 paths

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify victims with large locked balances approaching unlock
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Call lockOnBehalf(activeToken, 0, victim) — costs only gas
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Victim's unlockTime resets to now + their lockDuration
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Repeat periodically to permanently prevent unlock. Automate via contract.

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Victim funds permanently frozen; protocol trust destroyed
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires contract upgrade — no admin function can fix individual locks
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All user withdrawals can be griefed

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Trivial — single transaction per griefing cycle

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: lockOnBehalf calls with 0 quantity from non-related addresses
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Repeated unlockTime resets for same user without proportional quantity increase
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Automated lockOnBehalf calls targeting whales near unlock

threats.title: Phantom Remainder Allows NFT Minting at Discounted Lock Amounts
threats.hypothesis_description: When a user unlocks all their tokens, lockedToken.remainder is not cleared. In a subsequent lockdrop lock, _lock adds this phantom remainder to the new quantity for NFT calculation: `quantity = _quantity + lockedToken.remainder`. This allows users to receive NFT reveals backed by tokens that are no longer locked, effectively minting NFTs at below the configured nftCost.
threats.investigation_rationale: The unlock function only decrements quantity and does not touch remainder. The remainder from a prior lockdrop persists indefinitely, creating unbacked NFT minting credit. Combined with OPS-H001, a user could unlock early within the same lockdrop and re-lock to amplify the exploit.

proof_of_signal.pattern: `quantity = _quantity + lockedToken.remainder` uses stored remainder from prior lockdrop; unlock() at line 345 only decrements quantity, never clears remainder

preconditions: User accumulated a non-zero remainder during a lockdrop
User can unlock tokens (unlock time passed or exploited via OPS-H001)
A subsequent lockdrop period begins (or same lockdrop if using OPS-H001)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: unlock() does not reset remainder; subsequent _lock() re-uses it for NFT calculation

operational_questions: What is the value of NFTs relative to the nftCost to quantify economic impact?
Can this be combined with OPS-H001 for same-lockdrop exploitation?
Does the remainder compound across multiple lockdrop cycles?
validation_requirements: Lock 150 tokens during lockdrop (nftCost=100), verify remainder=50
Unlock all 150, verify remainder still equals 50
Lock 60 tokens in next lockdrop, verify 1 NFT minted (60+50=110 >= 100)

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Lock amount during lockdrop to maximize remainder (e.g., lock 199 with nftCost=100 → remainder=99)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Wait for unlock time (or exploit OPS-H001 to unlock early)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Unlock all tokens, retaining phantom remainder
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Re-lock minimal amount (just 1 token) in next lockdrop → gets 1 NFT from remainder

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: NFT supply inflation, lockdrop reward system integrity compromised

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Low — standard user interactions across lockdrop periods

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Users with quantity=0 but non-zero remainder
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Small lock amounts generating disproportionate NFT reveals

threats.title: Locking Outside Lockdrop Period Destroys Accumulated NFT Remainder
threats.hypothesis_description: In _lock, the local variable `remainder` is initialized to 0. The NFT calculation block (which computes a new remainder) is only executed during active lockdrop periods. Outside lockdrop, `lockedToken.remainder = remainder` unconditionally overwrites the stored remainder with 0, permanently destroying the user's accrued partial-NFT credit.
threats.investigation_rationale: Users who lock additional tokens between lockdrop periods lose their accumulated remainder without any warning. This is a silent value destruction that affects honest users performing normal operations.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Local `uint256 remainder;` defaults to 0; only set inside `if (lockdrop.start <= block.timestamp && lockdrop.end >= block.timestamp)` block; `lockedToken.remainder = remainder` always executes

preconditions: User has non-zero remainder from a previous lockdrop lock
User calls lock/lockOnBehalf outside of an active lockdrop period

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Remainder is unconditionally overwritten regardless of lockdrop activity
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None — happens to any user locking outside lockdrop
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time outside active lockdrop period

operational_questions: How many users have accumulated non-zero remainders?
Is there a user-facing warning about remainder loss?
Could an attacker use lockOnBehalf to zero a victim's remainder?
validation_requirements: Lock during lockdrop, accumulate remainder, lock again outside lockdrop, verify remainder zeroed

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Call lockOnBehalf(token, 0, victim) outside lockdrop period
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Victim's remainder is zeroed, losing NFT progress

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Users lose partial NFT credit, reducing trust
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Cannot restore lost remainder without contract upgrade

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: lockOnBehalf calls outside lockdrop targeting users with remainder
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Remainder values dropping to zero unexpectedly

threats.title: Price Feed Operator Can Both Disapprove and Approve Same Proposal
threats.hypothesis_description: approveUSDPrice checks only `approvals[msg.sender] == _usdProposalId` to prevent double-voting, but does not check `disapprovals[msg.sender]`. A price feed operator who disapproved can subsequently call approveUSDPrice, incrementing both approvalsCount and disapprovalsCount. This reduces effective multisig security from 3-of-5 to 2-of-5 and can cause proposals to become stuck (enough approvals but phantom disapprovals block execution).
threats.investigation_rationale: The _execUSDPriceUpdate function requires `approvalsCount >= APPROVE_THRESHOLD AND disapprovalsCount < DISAPPROVE_THRESHOLD`. When operators inflate both counters, legitimate proposals with 3 approvals may not execute because disapprovalsCount also reached the threshold. A proposal with proposer + 2 disapprove-then-approve voters would have 3 approvals and 2 disapprovals — stuck if DISAPPROVE_THRESHOLD=2.

proof_of_signal.pattern: approveUSDPrice checks `approvals[msg.sender] == _usdProposalId` but NOT `disapprovals[msg.sender] == _usdProposalId`; disapproveUSDPrice checks approvals but allows subsequent approve

preconditions: Active USD price proposal exists
Operator has PriceFeed role (1-5)
Operator is not the proposer (proposer cannot approve again)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: approvals and disapprovals mappings are independent — no mutual exclusion enforced
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires PriceFeed role but exploits code bug in vote counting
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time a proposal is active

operational_questions: With default thresholds (3/3), can this permanently stuck a proposal?
Can this reduce the effective security of price updates to 2-of-5?
What downstream systems depend on accurate USD price updates?
validation_requirements: Propose price, have operator disapprove then approve, verify both counts increment
Test scenario where 3 approvals + 2 disapprovals blocks execution

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Colluding operator calls disapproveUSDPrice (disapprovalsCount=1) then approveUSDPrice (approvalsCount=2)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Second colluding operator does same (disapprovalsCount=2, approvalsCount=3)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: _execUSDPriceUpdate: approvalsCount=3>=3 BUT disapprovalsCount=2 is NOT < 2 → execution blocked. Proposal stuck.

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Price updates can be permanently blocked OR passed with fewer legitimate approvals
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires admin to delete proposal (no such function exists) or wait for natural resolution
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: USD pricing, weighted value calculations, reward distribution

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Same address appearing in both approval and disapproval events
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Proposals with high combined approval+disapproval counts
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Disapprove followed by approve from same address

threats.title: Fee-on-Transfer Token Accounting Insolvency Leading to Fund Loss
threats.hypothesis_description: The _lock function records `lockedToken.quantity += _quantity` without verifying actual tokens received via balance delta. For fee-on-transfer tokens, the contract receives fewer tokens than recorded. Over time, the sum of recorded quantities exceeds actual contract balance, causing later unlockers to be unable to withdraw — permanent fund loss for last users.
threats.investigation_rationale: Project documentation explicitly states the contract 'is expected to handle non-standard ERC20 behaviors, including fee-on-transfer.' The code does not implement balance-before/after checking. The configure-token-test.ts shows USDB and WETH configured; if any future token has transfer fees, insolvency results.
threats.config_evidence: configure-token-test.ts shows USDB/WETH/ETH configured. Documentation states fee-on-transfer tokens should be supported.

proof_of_signal.pattern: `token.transferFrom(_tokenOwner, address(this), _quantity)` followed by `lockedToken.quantity += _quantity` without balance delta check

preconditions: A fee-on-transfer token is configured as active in configuredTokens
Multiple users lock the token over time

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Quantity recorded equals _quantity parameter, not actual received amount
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires admin to configure a fee-on-transfer token
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Continuous once such token is configured

operational_questions: Are any currently configured tokens fee-on-transfer?
What future tokens might be added?
How quickly does insolvency grow with each lock?
validation_requirements: Deploy with fee-on-transfer token, lock from multiple users, verify last user cannot unlock full amount

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Admin configures fee-on-transfer token (or attacker exploits existing one)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Users lock tokens — each lock records more than actually received
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Early unlockers withdraw full recorded amount (possible while contract has balance)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Last unlockers find insufficient balance — permanent fund loss

potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: No recovery mechanism — funds simply don't exist in contract
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All unlock operations for the affected token

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: None for last-user loss; low for deliberate exploitation
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Grows over time with each lock

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Contract token balance < sum of all locked quantities
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Unlock failures for valid locked amounts

threats.title: ETH Unlock Permanently Fails for Smart Contract Wallet Users via transfer() Gas Limit
threats.hypothesis_description: The unlock function sends ETH via `payable(msg.sender).transfer(_quantity)` which forwards only 2300 gas. Smart contract wallets (Gnosis Safe, account abstraction wallets) commonly require more than 2300 gas to process incoming ETH. Users who locked ETH from such wallets will have their funds permanently frozen — unlock will always revert.
threats.investigation_rationale: On Blast, smart contract wallets are increasingly common. The 2300 gas stipend is insufficient for multisig receive handlers. Since there is no alternative withdrawal mechanism (no admin rescue function for ETH), affected funds are permanently locked. This directly violates the stated invariant: 'Funds must not get locked forever.'

proof_of_signal.pattern: `payable(msg.sender).transfer(_quantity)` — 2300 gas stipend insufficient for contract wallet receive()

preconditions: User's address is a smart contract wallet (e.g., Gnosis Safe)
User has locked ETH (tokenContract == address(0))
Wallet's receive/fallback requires > 2300 gas

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Using .transfer() instead of .call{value}() for ETH sends
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: No admin function to rescue stuck ETH
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent — any ETH locked from contract wallets is irrecoverable

operational_questions: What percentage of registered players use smart contract wallets on Blast?
Is there any admin rescue function for stuck ETH? (No evidence of one)
Does Blast's ETH yield mechanism affect receive() gas cost?
validation_requirements: Lock ETH from Gnosis Safe, attempt unlock, verify revert
Confirm no alternative withdrawal path exists

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: User locks ETH from smart contract wallet (normal usage)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Lock period expires, user calls unlock()
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: transfer() reverts due to insufficient gas at receiver
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: User's ETH is permanently frozen — no recovery path

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Permanent ETH loss for contract wallet users
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: ETH unlock for all smart contract wallet users

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Any ETH lock from contract wallet

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: ETH locked but never unlocked from specific addresses

threats.title: Zero Lock Duration Enables Instant Lock-Unlock for Reward Gaming
threats.hypothesis_description: When no lockdrop is active and a user's playerSettings.lockDuration is 0, the fallback `_lockDuration = lockdrop.minLockDuration` may also be 0 (if no lockdrop has been configured or lockdrop.minLockDuration was set to 0). Outside lockdrop, there is NO validation of _lockDuration. This results in unlockTime = block.timestamp, allowing immediate unlock. Users can repeatedly lock/unlock to trigger forceHarvest for schnibbles rewards without any actual lock commitment.
threats.investigation_rationale: setLockDuration has no minimum bound check (only maximum). _lock outside lockdrop performs no duration validation. If the lockdrop struct has minLockDuration=0 (default), zero-duration locks are possible.
deployment_assumptions: lockdrop.minLockDuration is 0 or no lockdrop has been configured
threats.config_evidence: lockdrop struct is default-zero until configureLockdrop is called; start-lockdrop.ts configures it but minLockDuration value depends on startLockdrop action parameters

proof_of_signal.pattern: `if (_lockDuration == 0) { _lockDuration = lockdrop.minLockDuration; }` — if lockdrop.minLockDuration is also 0, no duration enforced outside lockdrop; no minimum validation follows

preconditions: User has not set a non-zero lockDuration via setLockDuration
lockdrop.minLockDuration == 0 (no lockdrop configured or minLockDuration set to 0)
Current time is outside active lockdrop period
Token is configured and active

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: No minimum duration validation outside lockdrop period

operational_questions: What is lockdrop.minLockDuration after deployment and between lockdrop events?
Can forceHarvest be profitably gamed with zero-duration locks?
Does setLockDuration(0) effectively create a permanent zero-duration setting?
validation_requirements: Deploy without configuring lockdrop, lock with fresh user, verify unlockTime == block.timestamp
Verify unlock succeeds in same/next block

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Lock tokens outside lockdrop — unlockTime = block.timestamp
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: forceHarvest triggered in _lock, crediting schnibbles for the locked amount
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Unlock immediately in same block. Repeat to farm schnibbles.

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Schnibbles reward inflation, unfair advantage over genuine lockers
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Schnibbles reward distribution, lock incentive mechanism

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Repeated lock/unlock in same block from same address
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: High schnibbles accumulation with minimal lock duration

threats.title: Missing SafeERC20 Causes DoS for Non-Standard Token Interactions
threats.hypothesis_description: The contract uses raw `IERC20.transfer()` and `IERC20.transferFrom()` without SafeERC20 wrappers. Tokens that don't return a boolean (like USDT on some chains) will cause these calls to revert due to ABI return data decoding failure in Solidity 0.8.25. This permanently prevents locking or unlocking such tokens, potentially freezing already-locked funds.
threats.investigation_rationale: Project documentation explicitly states the contract should handle 'missing return values' tokens. Solidity 0.8's ABI decoder requires the expected return type to match; a void-returning transfer will revert. If such a token is configured and users lock it before the issue is discovered, funds become permanently frozen.
deployment_assumptions: A non-standard ERC20 token (no bool return on transfer) is configured
threats.config_evidence: configure-token-test.ts configures USDB/WETH/ETH which are standard on Blast; however documentation states future additions and non-standard token support is expected

proof_of_signal.pattern: Raw `token.transferFrom(_tokenOwner, address(this), _quantity)` and `token.transfer(msg.sender, _quantity)` without SafeERC20 wrapper

preconditions: Admin configures a token that does not return bool on transfer/transferFrom
Users attempt to lock or have already locked such tokens

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Direct IERC20 calls without SafeERC20 in Solidity 0.8.25
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires admin to configure non-standard token
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent once non-standard token is configured and locked

operational_questions: Are any planned future tokens non-standard return value?
Can unlock() still work if lock succeeded (lock uses transferFrom, unlock uses transfer)?
Does Blast's USDT implementation return bool?
validation_requirements: Configure a non-returning-bool token, attempt lock, verify revert
If lock somehow succeeds, attempt unlock, verify revert (funds frozen)

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Admin configures non-standard token based on documentation support claim
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Users lock the token — transferFrom reverts due to return data decoding
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: If lock succeeds through other means, unlock reverts — funds permanently frozen
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None — occurs as part of normal operations

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Token becomes unusable, potential permanent fund freeze
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires contract upgrade to add SafeERC20

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Reverted lock/unlock transactions for specific tokens
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Token configured but no successful locks

threats.title: proposeUSDPrice Does Not Trigger Execution When APPROVE_THRESHOLD=1
threats.hypothesis_description: The proposer auto-approves in proposeUSDPrice (approvalsCount=1), but _execUSDPriceUpdate is only called inside approveUSDPrice, never in proposeUSDPrice. If admin sets APPROVE_THRESHOLD=1 via setUSDThresholds, the proposer's auto-approval meets the threshold but execution never triggers. A second operator must still call approveUSDPrice, requiring minimum 2 approvals regardless of configured threshold. This breaks the governance semantics.
threats.investigation_rationale: setUSDThresholds allows threshold=1. proposeUSDPrice increments approvalsCount to 1 but doesn't check against threshold or call _execUSDPriceUpdate. The threshold is only evaluated in approveUSDPrice. This means APPROVE_THRESHOLD=1 actually requires 2 approvals.
threats.config_evidence: setUSDThresholds has no minimum bound check; admin could set threshold to 1

proof_of_signal.pattern: proposeUSDPrice sets approvalsCount=1 via `usdUpdateProposal.approvalsCount++` but never calls `_execUSDPriceUpdate()`; threshold check only in approveUSDPrice

preconditions: APPROVE_THRESHOLD is set to 1 by admin
Price feed operator proposes a new USD price

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: _execUSDPriceUpdate called only in approveUSDPrice, not in proposeUSDPrice

operational_questions: Has admin ever set APPROVE_THRESHOLD to 1?
Does the operator workflow documentation assume threshold=1 should auto-execute?
Are there other threshold values that cause unexpected behavior?
validation_requirements: Set APPROVE_THRESHOLD=1, propose price, verify proposal is NOT executed
Verify second operator approval is required to trigger execution

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Admin sets APPROVE_THRESHOLD=1 expecting single-operator price updates
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Operator proposes price — approvalsCount reaches 1 but no execution
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Price update is stuck, requiring unexpected second approval
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Stale prices persist, affecting weighted value calculations and rewards

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Price updates delayed or stuck with unexpected approval requirements
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Set threshold to 2+ to match actual behavior
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: USD price governance, weighted value calculations

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Proposals stuck with approvalsCount >= threshold but not executed
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Price updates not executing despite meeting threshold

threats.title: setUSDThresholds Allows Zero or Extreme Values Breaking Price Governance
threats.hypothesis_description: setUSDThresholds has no bounds validation on _approve or _disapprove values. Admin can set DISAPPROVE_THRESHOLD=0, which makes `disapprovalsCount < 0` always false for uint8 (impossible condition), permanently blocking ALL price update executions in _execUSDPriceUpdate. Conversely, setting APPROVE_THRESHOLD=0 allows unintended behavior since no approval count can be less than 0 for uint8.
threats.investigation_rationale: The _execUSDPriceUpdate condition requires `disapprovalsCount < DISAPPROVE_THRESHOLD`. With DISAPPROVE_THRESHOLD=0, this is `uint8 < 0` which is always false, making it impossible to execute any price update regardless of approval count. No recovery path exists within the contract since setUSDThresholds requires no active proposal.

proof_of_signal.pattern: No minimum/maximum bounds on `_approve` or `_disapprove` parameters; _execUSDPriceUpdate requires `disapprovalsCount < DISAPPROVE_THRESHOLD` which is impossible when threshold=0

preconditions: Admin sets DISAPPROVE_THRESHOLD to 0 (or other extreme value)
No active proposal at time of threshold change

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: No bounds checking on uint8 threshold values
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent until admin corrects the threshold

operational_questions: What values have been historically set for thresholds?
Is there a multisig process protecting the admin key from misconfiguration?
Can the admin fix the issue by resetting thresholds (yes, if no active proposal)?
validation_requirements: Set DISAPPROVE_THRESHOLD=0, propose and approve price update, verify execution is impossible

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: All subsequent price proposals cannot execute: `disapprovalsCount < 0` is always false
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: USD prices become permanently stale, distorting all weighted value calculations
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Admin must realize the error and reset threshold (requires no active proposal)

potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Recoverable if admin is aware — set threshold back to valid value
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All USD price updates, weighted value calculations

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: None — simple function call with wrong parameter

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Price proposals never executing despite sufficient approvals
